meta.subtitle: Day 13 — Mojtaba Khamenei Demands Gulf Base Closures, Oil Holds Above $101, UN Condemns Iran Without Naming US–Israel
meta.contextNote: The conflict's second week closes with Iran's new supreme leader issuing his first directive, the UN Security Council passing a resolution that condemns Iran while omitting any reference to US–Israeli operations, and oil sustaining above $101 despite a historic strategic reserve release.

strategicHeader.headlineJudgment: Mojtaba Khamenei's first public directive — demanding US base closures across the Gulf or face attack — combined with oil sustaining above $101 despite a coordinated reserve release and a UN Security Council resolution that condemns Iran while omitting any reference to US–Israeli operations, confirms that both the military and diplomatic trajectories have locked into an escalation cycle with no active offramp.
strategicHeader.trajectoryRationale: Three converging signals reinforce the escalation trajectory. First, the new supreme leader's written statement commits Iran to continued Hormuz enforcement and threatens Gulf-based US installations, foreclosing the diplomatic space his appointment might have opened. Second, oil holding above $101 after an unprecedented multi-nation reserve release signals that markets have priced in sustained disruption rather than a short-duration event. The observable tripwire that would alter this trajectory is a credible ceasefire framework endorsed by both Washington and Tehran — no such framework exists as of this cycle.

flashPoints.headline: Iran's new supreme leader threatens Gulf US bases
flashPoints.detail: Mojtaba Khamenei issued his first statement since appointment, demanding closure of US military bases across the Gulf or face Iranian attack, and affirming the Strait of Hormuz must remain a 'tool of pressure' against the coalition. The directive forecloses the possibility that leadership transition might produce a negotiating opening.

flashPoints.headline: Oil sustains above $101 despite historic reserve release
flashPoints.detail: Brent crude climbed above $101 per barrel despite a coordinated multi-nation strategic reserve release, indicating the market judges Hormuz disruption as structural rather than temporary. The US Energy Secretary stated Washington is 'simply not ready' for tanker escort operations through the Strait.

flashPoints.headline: UN confirms 3.2 million displaced across Iran
flashPoints.detail: UNHCR reported that 3.2 million people have been forcibly displaced within Iran since the campaign began, with the figure expected to rise as strikes continue across multiple Iranian cities. Al Jazeera documented residential destruction in Tehran neighbourhoods.

executive.bluf: Day 13 produced a leadership consolidation that narrows rather than opens diplomatic space: Mojtaba Khamenei's first directive committed Iran to continued Hormuz enforcement and threatened US Gulf installations, while oil sustained above $101 despite a coordinated reserve release. The UN Security Council condemned Iranian attacks on Gulf states in a resolution backed by 135 countries, but omitted any reference to US–Israeli operations — deepening Tehran's perception of institutional isolation. Three cargo ships were struck near Iran's coast, and the US Energy Secretary admitted Washington is 'simply not ready' for Hormuz escort operations.

keyJudgments.text: The air campaign has entered a sustained attrition phase across four theatres — Iran, Lebanon, Gulf maritime, and Israel — with no indication of operational pause.
keyJudgments.basis: Explosions reported for the 13th consecutive day across Iran and Israel; UNHCR confirmed 3.2 million displaced; Al Jazeera documented bunker-busters loaded at RAF Fairford and residential destruction in Tehran.

keyJudgments.text: Escalation has accelerated: Iran's new supreme leader rejected negotiations and threatened US Gulf bases, closing the diplomatic window that leadership transition might have opened.
keyJudgments.basis: Mojtaba Khamenei's written directive demanded base closures and affirmed Hormuz as a coercive tool; US intelligence sources told Reuters Iran remains stable and 'not in danger of collapse', removing incentive for coalition restraint.

keyJudgments.text: Energy markets now price Hormuz disruption as structural: oil holds above $101 despite a historic reserve release, and the US admits it cannot yet escort tankers through the Strait.
keyJudgments.basis: Brent crude sustained above $101 per barrel; US Energy Secretary Wright stated escort operations are 'simply not ready'; Maersk imposed emergency bunker surcharges.

keyJudgments.text: Reporting suggests the diplomatic landscape has fractured along a US-aligned axis: the UNSC condemned Iran without naming the coalition, Russia's counter-resolution failed, and Germany escalated domestic security posture.
keyJudgments.basis: Bahrain-tabled UNSC resolution passed with 135 co-sponsors condemning Iranian attacks on Gulf states, omitting mention of US–Israeli operations; Russia-backed resolution failed; Germany announced enhanced security for Israeli and US institutions.

keyJudgments.text: Reporting indicates, though corroboration is limited, that Iran's information blackout continues into week two while S&P flags heightened cyber-threat activity by affiliated groups.
keyJudgments.basis: Iran International reported ongoing wartime information blackout; S&P Global Ratings noted heightened activity by threat actors including DDoS attacks and phishing campaigns; no CISA or Five Eyes advisory has confirmed attribution.

keyJudgments.text: The balance of reporting points to the war risk insurance market entrenching crisis-level pricing: the IG P&I Clubs updated Persian Gulf cover terms and the GXL 2026/27 reinsurance structure has been finalised under conflict conditions.
keyJudgments.basis: International Group of P&I Clubs published an Iran/Persian Gulf cover update; GXL 2026/27 policy year reinsurance contract finalised; Marsh reported increased marine war risk raising P&I questions.

domains.title: BATTLESPACE · KINETIC
domains.assessmentQuestion: What is the current disposition of forces and military operations across all theatres in the last 24 hours?

keyJudgment.text: The air campaign has entered a sustained attrition phase across four theatres with no indication of operational pause, as confirmed by 13 consecutive days of strikes.
keyJudgment.basis: Al Jazeera documented explosions on the 13th consecutive day across Iran and Israel; UNHCR confirmed 3.2 million displaced; UK involvement confirmed via bunker-buster loading at RAF Fairford.

bodyParagraphs.text: Explosions continued for the 13th consecutive day across Iran, Israel, and several Middle Eastern states. An Israeli strike hit the Beirut beachfront, killing at least eight civilians and injuring dozens, according to Al Jazeera. UNHCR reported 3.2 million people displaced within Iran, with numbers expected to rise as strikes target residential areas. An Al Jazeera crew documented bunker-busting bombs loaded onto US military aircraft at RAF Fairford in the United Kingdom, confirming direct British logistical support for the air campaign.

The balance of reporting points to an expanding rather than contracting target set. Iran and Hezbollah confirmed coordinated retaliatory attacks on Israel, with a Hezbollah rocket striking a house in the Tel Aviv suburbs and a drone hitting a Dubai high-rise. The BBC reported US strikes on Iranian minelaying ships and three cargo vessels struck off Iran's coast, including one in the Strait of Hormuz. Russia and China continue to provide electronic warfare support and intelligence sharing to Tehran, eroding US–Israeli signals dominance in the Gulf theatre.

domains.title: ESCALATION · TRAJECTORY
domains.assessmentQuestion: What are the indicators of escalation, de-escalation, or horizontal expansion in the conflict?

keyJudgment.text: Escalation has accelerated: Mojtaba Khamenei's first directive rejected negotiations, threatened US Gulf bases, and affirmed Hormuz enforcement — closing the window that leadership transition might have opened.
keyJudgment.basis: Khamenei issued written demands for US base closures; US intelligence assessed Iran 'not in danger of collapse'; UNSC condemned Iran without naming the coalition, deepening Tehran's isolation.

bodyParagraphs.text: Mojtaba Khamenei's first public statement since becoming supreme leader demanded the closure of US military bases across the Gulf or face Iranian attack, and affirmed that the Strait of Hormuz 'should remain a tool of pressure'. Iran's UN envoy reported 1,348 civilians killed and 17,000 injured since the campaign began. Germany announced enhanced security for Israeli, Jewish, and US institutions, citing three security incidents since the war started. INTERCARGO issued a formal advisory urging heightened vigilance for seafarers as security risks escalate in the Gulf and Strait.

US intelligence sources assessed Iran as 'not in danger of collapse' and its leadership as retaining control, according to Reuters via Middle East Eye. This assessment removes a potential incentive for coalition restraint, given that regime fragility might otherwise encourage a negotiated pause. No credible ceasefire framework has been proposed by either belligerent.

The balance of reporting points to continued escalation. The new supreme leader's rhetorical posture mirrors his father's but carries the additional weight of a succession mandate to demonstrate strength. Iran International reported a legitimacy dilemma: Mojtaba Khamenei must project piety and power simultaneously, making de-escalation politically costly. The observable tripwire remains unchanged: a credible ceasefire framework endorsed by both Washington and Tehran. No such framework exists.

dissenterNote.text: The US intelligence assessment that Iran is 'not in danger of collapse' could alternatively signal a Washington pivot toward accepting a longer attritional campaign rather than seeking a knock-out escalation. If the regime is stable, the coalition may calculate that sustained pressure without escalation achieves the same strategic objectives, potentially flattening the escalation curve in weeks 3–4. The evidence for this counter-reading is thin but warrants monitoring.

domains.title: ENERGY · ECONOMIC
domains.assessmentQuestion: What is the disruption to energy flows, supply chains, or markets affecting Canadian interests?

keyJudgment.text: Energy markets now price Hormuz disruption as structural: oil sustains above $101 despite a historic reserve release, and the US admits escort operations are not yet feasible.
keyJudgment.basis: Brent crude held above $101; US Energy Secretary Wright said escort operations 'simply not ready'; Maersk imposed emergency bunker surcharges; Asia-Pacific markets fell on oil volatility.

bodyParagraphs.text: Brent crude climbed above $101 per barrel despite a coordinated multi-nation strategic petroleum reserve release, the largest since the 2011 Libya crisis. Asia-Pacific equity markets fell as oil volatility continued to drive risk-off sentiment. OPEC forecast global oil demand growth of 1.3 million barrels per day next year, adding upward pressure to an already tight market. Iran continues to route crude to China via alternative channels, bypassing Hormuz enforcement against other shippers.

Maersk imposed temporary emergency bunker surcharges across container routes. Ocean carriers introduced emergency fuel surcharges on key global trade lanes, with Indian rice exports to the Middle East facing delays and container shortages. Three cargo ships were struck off Iran's coast, including one in the Strait of Hormuz. The Port of Salalah in Oman reported fuel tank strikes. US Energy Secretary Wright stated Washington is 'simply not ready' to launch escort operations, adding: 'All of our military assets right now are focused on destroying Iran's offensive capabilities.'

Available evidence suggests Canadian energy producers benefit from sustained price premiums, but downstream consumers and import-dependent sectors face rising input costs. Gulf-state economic fracturing reported by Al Jazeera indicates broader trade disruption across routes that Canadian LNG and agricultural exports transit. DP World, which operates terminals at several Canadian-linked ports, posted record 2025 results — $24.4 billion revenue, up 22% — suggesting the logistics sector is absorbing conflict-driven demand reshuffling.

domains.title: DIPLOMATIC · POLITICAL
domains.assessmentQuestion: What are the positions of key state actors and diplomatic shifts signaling changed intent?

keyJudgment.text: Reporting suggests the diplomatic landscape has fractured along a coalition-aligned axis: the UNSC condemned Iran without naming the US or Israel, Russia's counter-resolution failed, and European states are tightening domestic security.
keyJudgment.basis: Bahrain-tabled UNSC resolution passed with 135 sponsors condemning Iranian attacks on Gulf states; Russia-backed resolution mourning 'tragic loss' of lives failed; Germany announced enhanced security measures.

bodyParagraphs.text: The UN Security Council adopted a Bahrain-tabled resolution, co-sponsored by 135 countries, condemning Iranian attacks on Gulf states. The resolution omitted any reference to US or Israeli military operations, prompting Tehran's ambassador to denounce the Council's 'selective outrage'. A Russian-led counter-resolution mourning the 'tragic loss of lives' in the broader Middle East failed. China expressed regret at the failure of Moscow's text. Iran's UN envoy cited 1,348 civilians killed and 17,000 injured since operations began. Eight Muslim-majority foreign ministers condemned Israel's closure of Al-Aqsa Mosque during Ramadan as a 'flagrant violation of international law'.

Available evidence points to a complete absence of active mediation tracks. Middle East Eye reported that Ali Larijani, Iran's former parliamentary speaker, believed before the war that Washington was not seeking agreement in the Geneva nuclear talks — a framing now circulating in Iranian elite discourse as vindication of hardline resistance. US Senators urged a probe into strikes that killed children in Iran, signalling fractures within Washington's own consensus. The UAE detained a British tourist for filming Iranian missile strikes on Dubai, reflecting Gulf-state sensitivity to civilian documentation of the conflict.

actorMatrix.posture: Military-first — not ready for escort ops
actorMatrix.assessment: Prioritising strike campaign over maritime security

actorMatrix.changeSincePrevCycle: Sent 13 tonnes humanitarian aid via Azerbaijan

actorMatrix.assessment: Economic hedging while avoiding military alignment
Preparing for blowback rather than mediating

domains.title: CYBER · INFORMATION OPS
domains.assessmentQuestion: What are the observable cyber and information operations and likely target set?

keyJudgment.text: Reporting indicates, though corroboration is limited, that Iran's information blackout enters its second week while S&P flags heightened affiliated cyber-threat activity.
keyJudgment.basis: Iran International reported continued wartime blackout; S&P Global Ratings cited DDoS, phishing, and corporate network compromise attempts; no CISA or Five Eyes advisory has confirmed specific attribution.

bodyParagraphs.text: Iran's wartime information blackout entered its second week. Iran International reported the regime has intensified threats against domestic and diaspora critics. S&P Global Ratings flagged heightened activity by threat actors and affiliated hacktivist groups since the war's outbreak, including distributed denial-of-service attacks, phishing campaigns, and attempts to compromise corporate networks. The BBC examined the role of cyber warfare in the conflict, though specifics remain classified.

The information blackout likely serves dual purposes: domestic control and wartime operational security. France reported a foreign disinformation campaign targeting mayoral candidates — though attribution to Iran is unconfirmed. Germany's enhanced security posture reflects European concern about Iranian-directed operations extending beyond the military theatre. No CISA or Five Eyes advisory has confirmed specific Iranian cyber attacks on Western critical infrastructure this cycle.

domains.title: WAR RISK INSURANCE · MARITIME FINANCE
domains.assessmentQuestion: How is the insurance market pricing and responding to war risk in the Gulf, Red Sea, and Eastern Mediterranean?

keyJudgment.text: The balance of reporting points to the war risk market entrenching crisis pricing: the IG P&I Clubs updated Persian Gulf cover terms, the GXL 2026/27 reinsurance contract finalised under conflict conditions, and Marsh flagged rising P&I questions.
keyJudgment.basis: International Group of P&I Clubs published Iran/Persian Gulf cover update; GXL 2026/27 pooling and excess-of-loss structure finalised; Marsh issued advisory on marine war risk implications for P&I cover.

bodyParagraphs.text: The International Group of P&I Clubs published an update on war coverage for the Iran/Persian Gulf region, reflecting changes following both the attacks on Iran and Iran's retaliation against Gulf states hosting US military bases. The GXL reinsurance contract structure for the 2026/27 policy year has been finalised, embedding conflict-era risk assumptions into the annual renewal cycle. This structural change locks elevated pricing into the market regardless of short-term ceasefire prospects.

Marsh reported that increased marine war risk is raising P&I questions across the Lloyd's market. IUMI, Aon, Willis, and Munich Re all published updated marine risk advisories this cycle. Insurance Insider reported heightened underwriter activity. Ocean carriers' emergency fuel surcharges compound the cost burden on vessel operators already facing elevated war risk premiums.

Three cargo ships were struck off Iran's coast, including one in the Strait of Hormuz. The Port of Salalah in Oman reported fuel tank fires from strikes. Maersk imposed temporary emergency bunker surcharges. The World Shipping Council stated that the latest attacks on commercial shipping 'underline risk to seafarers and shipping in the Middle East.' Splash 247 published a rebuttal to President Trump's call for ships to have the 'guts' to cross Hormuz, noting the disconnect between political rhetoric and operational reality for commercial seafarers.

Available evidence points to Canadian marine insurers and P&I correspondents facing increased claim frequency as the GXL 2026/27 structure embeds conflict pricing. Canadian-flag or Canadian-interest vessels transiting the Gulf region face materially higher additional premium costs. The market signal is clear: underwriters expect sustained conflict, not a short-duration event.

warningIndicators.detail: Iran confirmed coordinated attacks with Hezbollah against Israel; Hezbollah rocket struck Tel Aviv suburbs; Israeli strikes continued across Beirut and Tehran for the 13th consecutive day. Status remains triggered from Cycle 010.

Three cargo ships struck near Iran's coast including one in the Strait; Mojtaba Khamenei affirmed Hormuz as 'tool of pressure'; US Energy Secretary stated escort operations 'simply not ready'. Disruption is structural.

Iran International reported IAEA says no damage at Iran nuclear sites, but Iran's envoy claimed Natanz was hit. Conflicting reporting prevents confirmation. Access status unclear amid ongoing strikes on Iranian territory.

Iran confirmed coordinated attacks with Hezbollah against Israel; massive destruction reported in Beirut neighbourhoods after Israeli strikes; Al Jazeera documented rubble filling streets in southern Beirut.

warningIndicators.indicator: US direct military engagement with Iran
warningIndicators.detail: BBC reported US strikes on Iranian minelaying ships. Bunker-busting bombs loaded onto US aircraft at RAF Fairford confirmed by Al Jazeera. US Energy Secretary confirmed all military assets focused on destroying Iran's offensive capabilities.

Iran International confirmed the wartime information blackout enters its second week, now extending beyond initial crisis management into sustained censorship. Mojtaba Khamenei's first statement was issued in writing only, suggesting communications infrastructure limitations even within the leadership.

Iran International reported IAEA says no damage at nuclear sites, but Iran's envoy claimed Natanz was struck. Conflicting reporting persists from Cycle 010. A Tier 1 confirmation from IAEA would trigger this indicator.

warningIndicators.indicator: Sovereign credit downgrade or insurance market withdrawal
warningIndicators.detail: No formal withdrawal, but the GXL 2026/27 reinsurance structure finalised under conflict conditions embeds elevated pricing. Marsh and S&P flagged rising P&I and cyber risk exposure. A named insurer pulling Gulf cover entirely would trigger this indicator.

collectionGaps.gap: AP and Reuters blocked — both returned HTTP 403/401
collectionGaps.significance: Removes the two primary Tier 1 wire services from the collection baseline, preventing independent confirmation of casualty figures and operational claims from both belligerents.

collectionGaps.gap: CENTCOM and IDF Spokesperson feeds failed to return content
collectionGaps.significance: Eliminates official US and Israeli military source reporting. Coalition operational claims cannot be independently verified against their own statements this cycle.

collectionGaps.gap: No real-time Brent crude spot price or Platts pricing data ingested
collectionGaps.significance: Oil price references rely on Anadolu Agency reporting ($101+) rather than direct market data. Prevents precise quantification of price movements and spread analysis.

collectionGaps.gap: No CISA advisories or Five Eyes cyber threat alerts collected (feed returned empty)
collectionGaps.significance: Prevents attribution confirmation for reported cyber activity. S&P's threat assessment cannot be cross-referenced with government indicators.

collectionGaps.gap: Lloyd's List blocked (HTTP 403); Clarksons Research and Dryad Global feeds failed
collectionGaps.significance: Removes three specialist maritime intelligence sources. War risk premium quantification relies on IG P&I Clubs and broker advisories rather than direct market data.

items.label: SOURCE DEPENDENCY
items.text: AP and Reuters were unavailable this cycle. All Tier 1 wire-service sourcing relies on Al Jazeera English, which may carry editorial framing differences. CENTCOM and IDF Spokesperson feeds also failed.

items.label: IRANIAN CLAIMS
items.text: Iranian government casualty figures (1,348 killed, 17,000 injured) and Mojtaba Khamenei's directives are treated as 'claimed' — Iranian state media assertions are never factual input.

items.label: NUCLEAR STATUS
items.text: Conflicting reports on Natanz damage persist: IAEA says no damage, Iran's envoy claims a strike. This brief does not resolve the contradiction.

items.label: OIL PRICING
items.text: No direct market data was ingested. The $101+ figure is sourced from Anadolu Agency reporting, not real-time commodity feeds.

caveats.confidenceAssessment: Overall confidence is MODERATE-HIGH. Strong Tier 1 coverage from Al Jazeera, CTP-ISW, IAEA, IG P&I Clubs, BIMCO, and UKMTO. Weakened by absence of AP, Reuters, CENTCOM, and IDF Spokesperson. Diplomatic and escalation assessments rely predominantly on Tier 2 sources (Middle East Eye, Iran International, Anadolu Agency).

dissenterNotes.text: The US intelligence assessment that Iran is 'not in danger of collapse' could alternatively signal a Washington pivot toward accepting a longer attritional campaign rather than seeking a knock-out escalation. If the regime is stable, the coalition may calculate that sustained pressure without escalation achieves the same strategic objectives, potentially flattening the escalation curve in weeks 3–4. The evidence for this counter-reading is thin but warrants monitoring.

caveats.sourceQuality: 509 items ingested from 63 of 73 sources. Tier 1: 17 sources OK, 2 failed (AP, Reuters). Tier 2: 36 OK, 8 failed. Tier 3: 10 OK, 0 failed.
